title: Inheritance
summary: Learn how classes can share and reuse code by inheriting from a parent class, so you write less and do more.

blocks.md:
Imagine you are designing a zoo app. You have dogs, cats, and parrots. All of them have a name, make sounds, and need to eat. If you wrote a completely separate class for each animal, you would repeat the same code over and over.

**Inheritance** solves this. You write the shared stuff once in a **parent class**, then each animal gets its own class that automatically *inherits* everything from the parent. Less typing, fewer bugs, and cleaner code.
Start with a general `Animal` class. Every animal has a `name` and can `eat`. This class becomes the *blueprint that other classes build on top of*.

blocks.caption: The parent class — Animal — holds everything all animals share.

blocks.md: To inherit from `Animal`, put the parent class name inside the parentheses when defining the child class: `class Dog(Animal)`. This one line gives `Dog` *all* of `Animal`'s attributes and methods for free.

blocks.title: The "is-a" relationship
blocks.md: Ask yourself: **is a Dog an Animal?** Yes! That's exactly when inheritance makes sense. A `Dog` *is-a* `Animal`, so it should inherit from `Animal`. You would not make `Leash` inherit from `Animal` because a leash is not an animal.

blocks.code:
class Dog(Animal):
    pass  # Nothing extra yet — Dog inherits everything from Animal

fido = Dog("Fido")
fido.eat()
fido.speak()
blocks.output:
Fido is eating.
Fido makes a sound.
blocks.caption: Dog inherits eat() and speak() without us writing them again.

blocks.text: Calling super().__init__() to Set Up the Parent

blocks.md: When a child class needs its own `__init__` (for example, to store extra data), you must also run the parent's `__init__`. You do that with `super().__init__(...)`. Think of `super()` as a polite way of saying: "Hey parent, please do your setup first."

blocks.code:
class Dog(Animal):
    def __init__(self, name, breed):
        super().__init__(name)   # runs Animal.__init__ to set self.name
        self.breed = breed       # then we add the Dog-specific attribute

fido = Dog("Fido", "Labrador")
print(fido.name)
print(fido.breed)
fido.eat()
blocks.caption: super().__init__ handles the parent's setup; we only add what's new.

blocks.title: Forgetting super().__init__() breaks self.name
blocks.md:
If you define `__init__` in the child class but forget to call `super().__init__()`, the parent's setup code never runs. Attributes like `self.name` that the parent sets will be missing, and you will get an `AttributeError`.

Always call `super().__init__(...)` as the **first line** of the child's `__init__`.

A `Dog` doesn't just "make a sound" — it barks. A `Cat` meows. You can **override** a parent method by defining a method with the *same name* in the child class. Python will use the child's version instead of the parent's.

blocks.code:
class Dog(Animal):
    def __init__(self, name, breed):
        super().__init__(name)
        self.breed = breed

    def speak(self):  # overrides Animal.speak
        print(f"{self.name} says: Woof!")


class Cat(Animal):
    def speak(self):  # overrides Animal.speak
        print(f"{self.name} says: Meow!")


fido = Dog("Fido", "Labrador")
whiskers = Cat("Whiskers")

fido.speak()
whiskers.speak()
whiskers.eat()  # inherited from Animal — not overridden
blocks.output:
Fido says: Woof!
Whiskers says: Meow!
Whiskers is eating.
blocks.caption: Each subclass overrides speak() while still sharing eat() from Animal.

blocks.text: Using super() to Extend a Method

blocks.md: Sometimes you want to *add* to what the parent does, not completely replace it. Call `super().method_name()` inside the child's override, then add your own code on top.

blocks.caption: super().eat() runs the parent's logic; then we tack on dog-specific behaviour.

blocks.md: Here is the complete example bringing everything together: one `Animal` parent and two child classes, each sharing common code while being unique in their own way.

blocks.output:
Fido says: Woof!
Fido is eating.
Whiskers says: Meow!
Whiskers is eating.
blocks.caption: Looping over different types that share a common interface — this is the power of inheritance.

blocks.title: Check relationships with isinstance()
blocks.md:
Python lets you verify the "is-a" relationship at runtime:

```python
print(isinstance(fido, Dog))    # True
print(isinstance(fido, Animal)) # True — Dog is-a Animal
print(isinstance(fido, Cat))    # False
```

This is handy when you need to know what kind of object you are working with.

blocks.question: You define `class Bird(Animal)` and give it its own `__init__(self, name, can_fly)`. What should the first line inside that `__init__` be?
blocks.explanation: Calling `super().__init__(name)` runs `Animal.__init__` so that `self.name` is properly set by the parent. If you skip it and just write `self.name = name` yourself it might work in this simple case, but you lose the parent's full setup logic and the code becomes fragile and harder to maintain.

keyTakeaways: A child class inherits all attributes and methods from its parent class — write shared code once.
Use `class Child(Parent):` to create a subclass; this expresses the "is-a" relationship.
Always call `super().__init__(...)` inside the child's `__init__` so the parent can do its own setup.
Override a parent method by defining one with the same name in the child — Python uses the child's version.
Use `super().method()` inside an override when you want to extend, not completely replace, the parent's behaviour.

exercise.prompt:
Create a `Vehicle` parent class with an `__init__` that stores `make` and `model`, and a method `describe()` that prints something like `"Toyota Camry"`. Then create two subclasses:
- `Car(Vehicle)` — add a `doors` attribute and override `describe()` to also print the number of doors.
- `Motorcycle(Vehicle)` — add a `has_sidecar` boolean and override `describe()` to mention whether it has a sidecar.
Create one instance of each and call `describe()` on both.
exercise.hint: In each child's `__init__`, call `super().__init__(make, model)` first, then store the extra attribute. In each `describe()`, you can call `super().describe()` to print the make/model, then add the extra detail.
